sentences.base_language: I wake up at seven o'clock.
sentences.grammarNote: Time expressions use the particle 에 (e) to mark a specific point in time: 일곱 시에 (at seven o'clock). Relative time words like 오늘 (today) and 매일 (every day) do NOT use 에 — they stand alone.
그 다음에 (geu daeume) means 'after that / then'. Korean sequences actions with connectors like 먼저 (first), 그 다음에 (then), 마지막으로 (finally), mirroring how time flows through a routine.
먹다 (meokda) is the base form of 'to eat'. In 해요-style speech: 먹어요 (meogeoyo). The object marker 을 (eul) follows 아침 because it ends in a consonant. After a vowel, use 를 instead.

sentences.base_language: I drink a cup of coffee.
sentences.grammarNote: 한 잔 (han jan) = one cup/glass. 잔 is the counter for cups of liquid. Korean counters follow the noun: 커피 한 잔 (one cup of coffee), 물 두 잔 (two glasses of water).

sentences.base_language: I leave the house at eight.
I take the subway to work.
sentences.grammarNote: 로/으로 (ro/euro) marks the means or direction: 지하철로 (by subway), 버스로 (by bus). Use 으로 after a consonant, 로 after a vowel or ㄹ. Destination uses 에: 회사에 가다 (go to the office).
